title: LLM Agents — ReAct, Tool Use, Reasoning & Self-Correction
description: At Staff level, loop engineering is about designing reliable multi-step LLM workflows: reasoning chains, tool use, agentic loops, and self-correction. These patterns enable LLMs to solve complex tasks that require planning, execution, and verification.

sections.title: Reasoning Patterns: CoT, ToT, GoT & Self-Consistency
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts the model to 'think step by step' before answering. This converts a direct-answer regression into a sequence of intermediate reasoning steps. Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) extends CoT by branching — at each step, the model generates K=3-5 candidate next thoughts, then evaluates their promise via heuristic scoring, and explores the most promising paths via BFS/DFS. Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT) generalises further — thoughts are nodes, edges are dependencies (aggregation, refinement), and reasoning becomes a directed graph that can be pruned and merged.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: CoT improves GSM8K by 15-25% over direct answer (from ~50% to ~75% for PaLM 540B). ToT on the Game of 24 benchmark improves from 7.3% (CoT) to 74% (ToT) by exploring 100 nodes with BFS. Self-consistency: sample K=5 CoT paths, take majority vote → 5-10% additional accuracy over single-path CoT. Cost: ToT is 5-10× more tokens than CoT per question. GoT adds 20-30% latency overhead over ToT due to graph operations.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Start with CoT (zero-shot: 'Let's think step by step'). 2) If accuracy plateaus below target, add self-consistency (K=5, majority vote → 5-10% gain). 3) For tasks requiring exploration (planning, puzzles, math proofs), upgrade to ToT with BFS (beam width=3, depth=5). 4) For multi-step interdependent reasoning (e.g., research synthesis), use GoT with explicit aggregation nodes. 5) Set a token budget: CoT = 512 tokens, ToT = 2K tokens, GoT = 4K+ tokens per query.

search Interview Angle: 'When would you use ToT over CoT?' CoT is sufficient for linear reasoning (GSM8K, date arithmetic). ToT is needed when the search space is combinatorial and early mistakes propagate — the tree structure lets the model backtrack from dead ends. Cost-benefit: test CoT+self-consistency first; only escalate to ToT if you're losing >10% accuracy to exploration failures.
sections.codeBlock:
# Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency, and Tree-of-Thoughts
import random

# === 1. Chain-of-Thought (few-shot) ===
COT_PROMPT = """Q: Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans, each with 3 balls. How many?
A: Roger starts with 5. 2 cans × 3 = 6 balls. 5 + 6 = 11. Answer: 11

Q: {question}
A:"""

def cot_answer(llm, question: str) -> str:
    return llm.generate(COT_PROMPT.format(question=question))

# === 2. Self-Consistency (majority vote over K samples) ===
def self_consistency(llm, question: str, K: int = 5, temperature: float = 0.7):
    answers = []
    for _ in range(K):
        raw = llm.generate(COT_PROMPT.format(question=question), temp=temperature)
        # Extract final "Answer: X" and normalise
        answer = raw.split("Answer:")[-1].strip()
        answers.append(answer)
    return max(set(answers), key=answers.count)  # majority vote

# === 3. Tree-of-Thoughts (BFS with pruning) ===
def tot_search(llm, problem: str, beam_width: int = 3, max_depth: int = 5):
    """BFS over thought tree. Each node: (state, value)."""
    frontier = [(problem, 0.0)]  # (current_state, heuristic_value)

    for depth in range(max_depth):
        candidates = []
        for state, _ in frontier:
            # Generate next K candidate thoughts
            thoughts = llm.generate(f"Given {state}, next step options:", N=beam_width)
            for thought in thoughts:
                # Heuristic: ask LLM to rate "promise" 0-1
                value = float(llm.generate(f"Rate 0-1: {thought} → solves {problem}?", temp=0.0))
                candidates.append((state + " | " + thought, value))

        # Prune to top-K by value
        candidates.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        frontier = candidates[:beam_width]

        # Early termination: check if any candidate is complete
        for state, value in frontier:
            if llm.generate(f"Is this solved? {state}", temp=0.0).strip() == "Yes":
                return state

    return frontier[0][0]  # Best partial solution

# ponytail: CoT is ~50 tokens, ToT is ~2K — 40× more. Use CoT+SC first.
# ToT upgrade path: BFS(3,5) → DFS with backtracking → GoT with aggregation

sections.title: Tool Use & Function Calling
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLMs are reasoning engines, not databases — they should call tools to fetch real-time data, execute computations, or interact with external systems. Tools are defined as JSON schemas (OpenAPI subset). The model outputs a structured JSON function call; your runtime dispatches it, captures the result, and feeds it back into the next model turn. Parallel tool calls invoke multiple independent tools in one turn (e.g., search + weather + calendar) — the model emits a list of calls in a single response. Sequential tool calls chain dependent tools where one call's output feeds the next (search → scrape → summarise). Choosing parallel vs sequential is the single biggest lever for agent latency.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Tool call latency: GPT-4 function call ~500ms to emit the call JSON + tool execution time. Parallel tool calls reduce latency from N×sequential to max(N tools) execution time. Typical budget: 1-3 tool calls per agent step, 5-15 tool calls total per task. Tool execution latency budget: real-time tools (<1s: search, calculator) vs async (10+s: PDF parsing, code execution sandbox). OpenAI function calling supports up to 128 tools per request. Anthropic tool use supports parallel tools in a single response.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define each tool's JSON schema (name, description, parameters, required fields). 2) System prompt includes tool definitions and the protocol: 'To call a tool, output {"function": "name", "args": {}...}'. 3) Runtime parses the function call, executes, and returns result as a 'tool' role message. 4) The model receives the result and either calls another tool or produces the final answer. 5) Set a max tool-call limit (e.g., 10) to prevent infinite loops. 6) For safety: validate tool arguments against schema before execution.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'How would you design a tool-calling system that handles 100+ tools?' 1) Group tools into categories (search, compute, database, external API). 2) Use a two-stage approach: router model selects top-5 relevant tools → main model calls from that subset. 3) Cache tool call patterns per task type to reduce routing latency. 4) Monitor tool call failure rates: if a tool fails >5% of calls, alert the team. 5) For 1000+ tools, use semantic retrieval over tool descriptions (embed + nearest neighbour) at each turn.

sections.title: Agentic Loops — ReAct Pattern
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: ReAct (Reasoning + Action) interleaves reasoning traces with tool calls. The canonical loop: Thought → Action → Observation → Thought. The model first thinks about what to do, then acts (calls a tool or produces an intermediate result), receives an observation (tool output), and reasons again about the next step. This explicit reasoning trace makes the agent's decisions interpretable and debuggable.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Max iterations: 5-15 per task (latency budget: 10-60 seconds). Beyond 15 iterations, agent loops tend to degrade — the model accumulates context noise and repeats failed strategies. Stopping conditions: 1) task complete (model emits 'Final:'), 2) max iterations reached, 3) error threshold exceeded (>3 tool failures in a row). Monitoring: steps per task (median 4-6 for web QA, 8-12 for multi-hop research), tool call success rate (target >90%), loop depth distribution (watch for long-tail >15 steps).

track_changes Production Loop Design: 1) Set hard max_iterations (default 10, configurable per task type). 2) Add a 'suicide' keyword: if the model outputs 'Give up: reason', gracefully degrade (log failure, return partial result). 3) Implement exponential backoff on tool retries (1s, 2s, 4s...). 4) Checkpoint state after each step (so a crashed agent can resume). 5) Budget-aware loop: track cumulative token consumption and force-stop if budget exceeded. 6) Timeout per step: if a model call takes >30s, kill and retry (or mark as failed).

search Interview Angle: 'How do you prevent agent loops from becoming infinite?' Three guards: 1) Hard iteration limit (10-15). 2) Observation deduplication: if the model repeats the same action with the same observation, increment a 'stuck' counter → kill after 3 repeats. 3) Semantic dead-end detection: if the last 3 thoughts don't change the plan (embedding similarity >0.95), force termination. These three layers catch >99% of runaway loops in production.
sections.codeBlock:
# ReAct agent loop — Thought → Action → Observation → Thought
import json, time
from typing import Optional

class ReActAgent:
    def __init__(self, llm, tools: list[dict], max_iters: int = 10, per_step_timeout: float = 30.0):
        self.llm = llm
        self.tools = tools
        self.max_iters = max_iters
        self.per_step_timeout = per_step_timeout

    def run(self, task: str) -> str:
        system = """You are a ReAct agent. Output one of:
- Thought: <your reasoning>
- Action: <tool_name>\nAction Input: <JSON args>
- Observation: <result>  # (provided by system)
- Final: <answer>

Available tools:""" + json.dumps(self.tools)

        messages = [{"role": "system", "content": system},
                    {"role": "user", "content": task}]
        stuck_counter = 0
        prev_action = None

        for step in range(self.max_iters):
            response = self.llm.chat(messages, timeout=self.per_step_timeout)
            content = response.content.strip()

            if content.startswith("Final:"):
                return content.replace("Final:", "").strip()

            if content.startswith("Action:"):
                lines = content.split("\n")
                action_name = lines[0].replace("Action:", "").strip()
                try:
                    action_input = json.loads("\n".join(lines[1:]).replace("Action Input:", "").strip())
                except json.JSONDecodeError:
                    action_input = {}

                # Stuck detection: same action repeated
                if (action_name, json.dumps(action_input)) == prev_action:
                    stuck_counter += 1
                    if stuck_counter >= 3:
                        return f"Stopped: repeated same action 3 times after {step} steps"
                else:
                    stuck_counter = 0
                prev_action = (action_name, json.dumps(action_input))

                # Execute tool
                try:
                    observation = self._execute_tool(action_name, action_input)
                except Exception as e:
                    observation = f"Error: {e}"

                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": content})
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": f"Observation: {observation}"})

            # else 'Thought:' — continue loop

        return "Stopped: max iterations reached after " + str(self.max_iters) + " steps"

    def _execute_tool(self, name: str, args: dict) -> str:
        # ponytail: real dispatch goes here; stub for illustration
        return f"Tool {name} returned stub result for {json.dumps(args)}"

# Production monitoring: log (step, thought, action, observation, latency)
# for every step. Alert if >3 steps with the same action pattern.

sections.title: Self-Correction & Reflection
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLMs cannot reliably detect their own errors during generation (they lack introspection). But they can evaluate and revise after seeing the full output. Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) follows a generate → feedback → refine loop: generate an initial output, get self-generated feedback ('What's wrong with this?'), then refine. Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) stores past failures as episodic memory: when the agent fails a task, it writes a 'reflection' into a memory buffer, and future episodes retrieve relevant reflections to avoid repeating mistakes.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Self-Refine correction success rates: 30-60% for code generation (HumanEval bug-fixing), 20-40% for reasoning (GSM8K error correction). Reflexion improves success rate on programming tasks by 20-40% over baseline ReAct. Cost: self-correction loops consume 2-3× more tokens per task (generate + critique + refine). Diminishing returns: >2 refinement rounds rarely improve quality (0-5% improvement per round after round 2).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Generate the initial answer. 2) Prompt for self-critique: 'Review your answer. List specific errors or omissions.' 3) Generate a revised answer incorporating the critique. 4) Optional: repeat steps 2-3 for one more round. 5) If the refined answer is worse (e.g., better score on held-out test), discard the refinement — use a validation step. 6) For Reflexion: after task completion (success or failure), summarise the trajectory into a 1-2 sentence 'lesson' and store in a vector memory for future retrieval.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'How would you design a self-correcting agent for code generation that minimises cost?' Two-tier: 1) Use a cheap verifier (lint + test results) to detect errors — only invoke the LLM for self-correction when the verifier fails. This reduces cost by 50-70% vs self-correction on every output. 2) Cache common corrections: if the same lint error pattern appears >10 times, write a rule-based fix (one-liner) instead of calling the LLM. 3) For the critique model, use a smaller/cheaper model (7B) while the generator is larger (70B) — 2-4× cost saving with <5% quality loss.
sections.codeBlock:
# Self-Refine and Reflexion patterns
from typing import Optional

class SelfRefine:
    def __init__(self, generator, critic):
        self.gen = generator  # Could be same model or smaller critic
        self.critic = critic

    def solve(self, problem: str, max_refinements: int = 2) -> str:
        # === Step 1: Generate initial answer ===
        answer = self.gen.generate(f"Solve: {problem}")

        for round_num in range(max_refinements):
            # === Step 2: Self-critique ===
            feedback = self.critic.generate(
                f"Problem: {problem}\nProposed: {answer}\n"
                f"List errors, omissions, or improvements:"
            )

            if "No issues found" in feedback:
                return answer  # Early exit — answer is good

            # === Step 3: Refine ===
            revised = self.gen.generate(
                f"Problem: {problem}\nPrevious: {answer}\n"
                f"Feedback: {feedback}\nRevised solution:"
            )

            # Validate: keep the better one (simple heuristic)
            if len(revised) > len(answer) * 0.5:  # Not trivially short
                answer = revised

        return answer


class ReflexionMemory:
    """Episodic memory for Reflexion agents."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.trajectories: list[dict] = []

    def record(self, task: str, success: bool, steps: list, reflection: str):
        self.trajectories.append({
            "task": task, "success": success,
            "steps": steps, "reflection": reflection,
        })

    def retrieve(self, task: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
        """Retrieve relevant reflections similar to this task.
        ponytail: uses keyword overlap; real system uses embeddings."""
        keywords = set(task.lower().split())
        scored = []
        for t in self.trajectories:
            overlap = len(keywords & set(t["task"].lower().split()))
            scored.append((overlap, t["reflection"]))
        scored.sort(reverse=True)
        return [r for _, r in scored[:top_k]]

# Reflexion agent loop:
# 1) Retrieve similar past reflections as context
# 2) Execute task with ReAct
# 3) After completion, write reflection to memory
# Success rate improves by 20-40% over plain ReAct for coding tasks.

sections.title: Multi-Agent Systems: Debate, Routing & Coordination
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Multiple agents can specialise, debate, and verify each other's work. Common patterns: Debate — two agents argue opposing views on the same question; a judge agent or majority vote resolves the conflict. Expert panels — domain-specific agents (coder, reviewer, tester) each contribute their expertise; a router assigns work to the right specialist. Router + workers — a lightweight router agent decomposes the task and delegates subtasks to worker agents, then aggregates results.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Coordination overhead: 2-5× latency for multi-agent vs single agent (agent communication + context passing). Use cases where multi-agent beats single-agent accuracy: fact-checking (+15-25% accuracy over single-agent RAG), code review (+30% bug detection), complex planning (+20% plan quality on TravelPlanner benchmark). Diminishing returns beyond 5 agents: 3-agent panels (coder + reviewer + tester) are the 'sweet spot' — more agents add overhead without accuracy gains.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define agent roles (coder, critic, researcher, planner). 2) Each agent gets a specialised system prompt with its persona and toolset. 3) Implement a shared message bus: agents post responses to a channel; other agents can read and reply. 4) A coordinator agent tracks progress, handles deadlocks (agent stuck in circular debate), and produces the final answer. 5) Set a max debate round limit (3-5 rounds) to bound cost. 6) For reliability: if an agent's output is consistently low-quality, degrade it (reduce its context window or switch to a simpler model).

search Interview Angle: 'Design a multi-agent system for automated code review.' Three agents: 1) Static analysis agent — runs linters (pylint, mypy, bandit), reports issues. 2) Security agent — reviews for OWASP Top-10, hardcoded secrets, injection vulnerabilities. 3) Reviewer agent — checks logic, readability, and adherence to code style guide. Coordinator aggregates their findings, deduplicates, and produces a PR review with severity ratings. The coder agent then addresses the top N issues and re-submits for review. This pattern catches 3× more bugs than any single agent.

sections.title: Production Agent Architecture: Queuing, State & Observability
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: In production, agent loops are not synchronous HTTP handlers — they are asynchronous workflows with queuing, persistence, and monitoring. Each agent step is a unit of work dispatched to a task queue. State is checkpointed after every step (so a crashed pod doesn't lose progress). A timeout handler kills loops that exceed their budget (e.g., >30s per step, >5min total). Observability collects step-level logs: latency, token count, tool calls, errors — surfaced via traces and dashboards. A dead agent without observability is just a black box burning API budget.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Agentic tasks cost 5-20× more than simple Q&A. A 10-step ReAct loop: ~10K input tokens + ~2K output tokens per step = ~120K tokens per task. At GPT-4 pricing ($30/M input, $60/M output): $0.0036 + $0.0072 = ~$0.01 per simple Q&A, vs $0.36 per agent task with 10 steps (36× more). Monitoring metrics: step duration (p50: 2-5s, p99: 20-30s), tool call success rate (target >95%), loop depth distribution (median 4-7, max 15), re-queue rate (<5%), partial completion rate (tasks that end without 'Final:' — target <10%).

track_changes Production Architecture: 1) Task queue (Redis/RabbitMQ/SQS) — enqueue agent tasks with priority. 2) Worker pool (Kubernetes pods or Lambda) — each worker runs one agent loop at a time. 3) State store (PostgreSQL or DynamoDB) — checkpoint agent state after each step: (session_id, step_number, messages, tool_results). 4) Rate limiter — per-tool token bucket (e.g., search API: 10 req/min, calculator: 100 req/min). 5) Timeout handler — if total duration > deadline (e.g., 5min), kill the loop and return partial result. 6) Observability — structured logs per step with trace_id encoding the full DAG; export to OpenTelemetry or Datadog.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'How do you handle an agent that calls seven different tools and the third call times out?' 1) Each tool call has its own timeout (default 10s, configurable per tool). 2) On timeout: retry once with exponential backoff (2s). 3) If retry fails: the agent receives 'Tool X timed out after 2 attempts' as observation. The agent then decides: try an alternative tool, skip this step, or abort with partial result. 4) The full state (including the timeout) is checkpointed so the user can resume or debug. 5) Alert if any tool times out >3 times in an hour → pager.
sections.codeBlock:
# Production agent — async queue, state persistence, observability
import json, time, uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class StepStatus(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    SUCCESS = "success"
    FAILED = "failed"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"

@dataclass
class AgentStep:
    step_id: str
    session_id: str
    step_number: int
    thought: str = ""
    action: str = ""
    action_input: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    observation: str = ""
    llm_latency_ms: float = 0.0
    tool_latency_ms: float = 0.0
    tokens_in: int = 0
    tokens_out: int = 0
    status: StepStatus = StepStatus.PENDING
    error: str = ""

class StateStore:
    def checkpoint(self, step: AgentStep):
        """Persist step to DB. Pseudocode; real impl: UPSERT into PostgreSQL."""
        pass

    def load_session(self, session_id: str) -> list[AgentStep]:
        """Resume a session (e.g., after pod crash)."""
        pass

class AgentWorker:
    def __init__(self, llm, tools: list, state_store: StateStore,
                 rate_limiter, total_timeout_s: float = 300.0):
        self.llm = llm
        self.tools = tools
        self.state = state_store
        self.rate_limiter = rate_limiter
        self.total_timeout = total_timeout_s

    def execute(self, task: str, session_id: str) -> str:
        start_time = time.time()
        session = self.state.load_session(session_id) or []
        step_number = len(session) + 1

        while time.time() - start_time < self.total_timeout:
            step = AgentStep(
                step_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
                session_id=session_id,
                step_number=step_number,
            )

            # === LLM call with timeout ===
            try:
                t0 = time.time()
                response = self.llm.chat([], timeout=30.0)
                step.llm_latency_ms = (time.time() - t0) * 1000
            except TimeoutError:
                step.status = StepStatus.TIMEOUT
                step.error = "LLM call timed out (>30s)"
                self.state.checkpoint(step)
                break

            # === Parse and execute action ===
            action = self._parse_action(response.content)
            step.thought = action.get("thought", "")
            step.action = action.get("action", "")
            step.action_input = action.get("input", {})

            if step.action == "final":
                step.status = StepStatus.SUCCESS
                self.state.checkpoint(step)
                return action.get("result", "")

            if step.action and self.rate_limiter.allow(step.action):
                t0 = time.time()
                try:
                    step.observation = self._execute_tool(step.action, step.action_input)
                    step.tool_latency_ms = (time.time() - t0) * 1000
                    step.status = StepStatus.SUCCESS
                except Exception as e:
                    step.status = StepStatus.FAILED
                    step.error = str(e)

            self.state.checkpoint(step)
            step_number += 1

        return "Partial result: agent timed out"

# Observability: export each step as OpenTelemetry span
# span.set_attribute("agent.step", step_number)
# span.set_attribute("tool.name", step.action)
# span.set_attribute("tool.success", step.status == StepStatus.SUCCESS)
# span.set_attribute("llm.tokens", step.tokens_in + step.tokens_out)

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (Yao et al., 2022) — The foundational ReAct agent pattern. Interleaves reasoning traces with tool actions.
  • Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning (Shinn et al., 2023) — Episodic memory for agents. Stores failure reflections and retrieves them to avoid repeating mistakes.
  • Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models (Yao et al., 2023) — BFS/DFS over thought nodes. Dramatic gains on tasks requiring exploration (Game of 24, crossword).
  • Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback (Madaan et al., 2023) — Generate → feedback → refine loop. 30-60% correction rate for code, 20-40% for reasoning.
  • Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools (Schick et al., 2023) — Self-supervised tool-use learning. Models learn to call APIs via few-shot examples without human annotation.
  • Graph of Thoughts: Solving Elaborate Problems with Large Language Models (Besta et al., 2023) — Generalises CoT/ToT to directed graphs. Merges, splits, and aggregates thought nodes.
  • OpenAI Function Calling Guide — Production function calling API. Tool definition as JSON schema, parallel tool calls, streaming.

details: Agentic System Design Flow: Start with ReAct (max 10 steps, CoT reasoning). If tool calls are needed, add function calling (parallel for independent tools). If accuracy is too low, add self-consistency (K=5 CoT paths, majority vote). If exploring decision trees, upgrade to ToT (BFS beam=3, depth=5). If agents need to learn from past failures, add Reflexion memory. If tasks need multiple perspectives, use a 3-agent panel (coder + reviewer + tester). Productionise with async queuing, state checkpointing, timeout handling, and step-level observability. Cost rises 5-20× from simple Q&A to agentic; monitor token budgets per step and enforce hard iteration limits. At FAANG scale, a 1% improvement in loop efficiency (reducing average steps from 6 to 5) saves $50K+/month on API costs for a system serving 1M queries/day.
